name: Stonehenge
summary: Stonehenge is a prehistoric monument consisting of a ring of standing stones, each around 13 feet high and weighing approximately 25 tons, set within a complex of earthworks on Salisbury Plain in Wiltshire. Constructed in several stages between approximately 3000 BC and 2000 BC, it remains one of the most mysterious and iconic ancient structures in the world. Its purpose, ranging from astronomical observatory to sacred burial ground, has been debated by scholars for centuries.

timeline.title: First Earthwork Enclosure
timeline.description: The earliest construction phase at Stonehenge begins with the digging of a circular ditch and bank, creating the henge itself. Within this enclosure, 56 pits known as the Aubrey Holes are dug, believed to have held timber posts or stones and later used for cremation burials.

timeline.title: Sarsen Stones and Bluestones Erected
timeline.description: The massive sarsen stones, transported from Marlborough Downs about 25 miles to the north, are erected in the iconic trilithon arrangement. The smaller bluestones, weighing up to 4 tons each, are brought from the Preseli Hills in Wales, an astonishing 150 miles away, a feat that remains one of the great engineering mysteries of prehistory.

timeline.title: Final Stone Arrangement
timeline.description: The stones are rearranged into their final configuration, with the great trilithon forming the inner horseshoe and the sarsen circle enclosing it. The Avenue, a processional route connecting Stonehenge to the River Avon, is also completed, suggesting the site had become a major ceremonial center.

timeline.title: Geoffrey of Monmouth's Legend
timeline.description: Geoffrey of Monmouth writes in his Historia Regum Britanniae that Stonehenge was built by the wizard Merlin, who magically transported the stones from Ireland. This medieval legend becomes the dominant explanation for the monument's origins for centuries and fuels its mystical reputation.

timeline.title: Duke of Buckingham's Excavation
timeline.description: The Duke of Buckingham sponsors the first known excavation at Stonehenge, digging several holes near the center of the monument. While the results are poorly documented, this marks the beginning of attempts to scientifically investigate the site's origins and purpose.

timeline.title: Astronomical Alignment Theory
timeline.description: Astronomer Gerald Hawkins publishes 'Stonehenge Decoded,' arguing that the monument functions as a sophisticated astronomical observatory for predicting eclipses and tracking the movements of the sun and moon. While controversial, his work ignites modern scientific interest in the site's alignments.

timeline.title: UNESCO World Heritage Status
timeline.description: Stonehenge, along with Avebury and associated sites, is designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site, recognizing its outstanding universal value. This triggers major changes in site management, including the closure of the road running alongside the monument and the construction of a new visitor center.

images.caption: Stonehenge during the great stone erection phase, with workers transporting and raising the massive sarsen trilithons
Medieval artistic depiction of Merlin magically raising the stones, inspired by Geoffrey of Monmouth's legend
Victorian-era photograph showing Stonehenge before modern conservation, with visitors freely walking among the stones
The restored Stonehenge with the modern visitor center and restricted access paths protecting the ancient monument
